behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: the failure boundary is the handoff between HTTP raw-body capture, signature verification, and enqueue call suppression, not just a single pure function. Rationale B: a unit test of HMAC comparison can pass while middleware wiring still forwards invalid events. REAL-INFRA does not require real network/OS; in-process app wiring is enough. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text only requires 401/no-forward semantics, not real network effects; Agentic/Workflow disqualified (no AI steps).
behaviors.test_description: Run the app in-process with real request parsing middleware and real verifier+enqueue modules. Send a signed-mismatch webhook and assert HTTP 401 plus zero queue writes/producer calls.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: this is an ordering guarantee across handler and queue persistence boundary. Rationale B: unit tests of handler logic can miss real ordering bugs if persistence is deferred or fire-and-forget. Integration-brake checks: no required real external network/subprocess; no AI output dependency; no multi-agent chain. Rejection gate: System rejected because local persistent store wiring (DB/filesystem) is sufficient per behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Use real queue persistence module (local DB/filesystem) with an instrumented hook/transaction log. Issue webhook, verify durable write completes before the HTTP 200 is observable.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: failure occurs at module interaction between request handler and async downstream worker invocation. Rationale B: isolated unit tests can miss accidental await/blocking introduced in orchestration. REAL-INFRA does not require real network; in-process server and controllable downstream future/promise is enough. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior targets async decoupling, not OS/network-specific failure.
behaviors.test_description: Start handler with real routing and a downstream processor that blocks on a controllable latch. Send request and assert response returns before latch release while event is still queued/processing.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: this is a wall-clock latency/concurrency SLO under contention, sensitive to runtime scheduling, socket handling, and real timing. Rationale B: mocked time or fully in-process fakes can hide head-of-line blocking and event-loop starvation seen only in realistic process/HTTP execution. REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both push to System. Rejection gate: Integration rejected because behavior explicitly requires wall-clock under concurrent load conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Run service as a real process, drive concurrent HTTP webhook traffic while earlier events trigger slow downstream work, and assert each new request gets a response within 3 seconds measured externally.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: dedup correctness is at state handoff between HTTP layer and persistent queue/idempotency store. Rationale B: unit tests of duplicate-check helper can pass while real persistence uniqueness/idempotency integration fails. No real external network/subprocess required; no AI dependency. Rejection gate: System rejected because local module+store integration is sufficient for duplicate write prevention.
behaviors.test_description: With real persistent queue/idempotency storage, submit same event ID twice via handler and assert only one durable queue record exists and second call returns expected idempotent behavior.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: behavior is mapping of queue-backpressure signal across module boundary into HTTP contract. Rationale B: a pure unit test can miss real error-type/metadata propagation mismatches between queue module and handler. Integration-brake: no required OS/network/subprocess effects beyond normal in-process request handling; no AI. Rejection gate: System rejected because this behavior does not require proving real capacity exhaustion mechanics (covered by behavior 7).
behaviors.test_description: Wire real handler to queue module interface (or real queue configured to emit full condition) and trigger a full error; assert HTTP 503 and correct Retry-After header value/format.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A: failure boundary is inside queue + persistence layer interaction (configured limit, durable state accounting, rejection path). Rationale B: mocking persistence can hide real off-by-one or transaction/locking bugs. REAL-INFRA check: local DB/filesystem-backed persistence fits Integration definition; no external process/network requirement stated. Rejection gate: System rejected because behavior text requires capacity enforcement correctness, not OS-level primitives beyond local store wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue with a fixed small capacity in local test storage, enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one more and assert deterministic rejection without corrupting existing queued entries.
